core_intuitions: The judge trades human-level fidelity for inference speed: it is an approximate, reproducible human proxy with systematic, correctable biases, and must never be treated as ground truth.

Pointwise scoring produces an absolute score per response (O(N) queries, weak cross-prompt anchor); pairwise scoring produces a relative winner (O(N^2) queries, better calibrated but position-biased). The relative judgment is cognitively easier and more reliable.

LLM judges deviate from humans in a small menagerie of predictable ways -- position, verbosity, self-preference, sycophancy, instruction-following -- and each has a specific mitigation. Knowing them is the central skill.
Position bias is cancelled by scoring BOTH orderings (A,B) and (B,A) and only crowning a winner when the two passes agree, otherwise declaring a tie.

Elo turns a stream of noisy individual pairwise battles into a stable global skill ranking: a logistic function maps the rating gap to an expected win probability, and each battle nudges ratings by the surprise (actual minus expected).
A judge is only trustworthy after it clears agreement / Spearman thresholds against a gold human-labeled set, and must be re-calibrated whenever the judge model changes.

new_figures.name: judge-pointwise-vs-pairwise
new_figures.concept: Side-by-side of the two evaluation paradigms: absolute rubric score for one response vs a winner picked between two responses, with their opposing tradeoffs.
new_figures.rationale: This is the organizing dichotomy of the whole chapter -- every later idea (position bias, Elo, calibration) attaches to one mode or the other. A reader who internalizes 'one response -> number, O(N)' vs 'two responses -> winner, O(N^2)' and WHY relative judgments are better calibrated has the scaffolding for everything that follows. The chapter currently conveys this only through prose plus a text table.
new_figures.placement: The Two Evaluation Paradigms: Pointwise vs Pairwise
new_figures.brief: A two-panel infographic, clearly divided down the middle with a thin vertical rule. LEFT panel titled 'Pointwise (absolute)': show a single response card (a small document glyph labeled 'Response') flowing by an arrow into a 'Judge' box, which flows into a vertical 1-to-5 rubric scale drawn as five stacked ticks with a filled marker at, say, 4 -- label the emitted value as a score badge reading 'score = 4'. Under it a compact caption line: 'one response -> a number'. RIGHT panel titled 'Pairwise (comparative)': show TWO response cards ('Response A', 'Response B') both feeding into one 'Judge' box, whose output is a winner ribbon reading 'A wins' (with a small 'or tie' note). Caption: 'two responses -> a winner'. Beneath both panels, a shared horizontal tradeoff strip with three labeled rows aligned under each panel using small paired indicators (use a filled/good dot vs a hollow/warn dot, NOT color words): Cost row -> pointwise 'O(N)' (good), pairwise 'O(N^2)' (warn); Calibration vs humans -> pointwise 'moderate' (warn), pairwise 'high' (good); Position bias -> pointwise 'low' (good), pairwise 'high' (warn). Use var(--accent) only for the emphasis on the emitted score badge and winner ribbon; use var(--good)/var(--warn) semantics for the tradeoff dots; keep boxes in var(--v-fill) with var(--v-stroke) borders and var(--ink-soft) text. Small illustrative integers only (scale 1-5, the marker at 4). Everything must fit; reading order left-to-right within each panel, top-to-bottom overall. No invented benchmark numbers.

new_figures.name: judge-bias-menagerie
new_figures.concept: The five systematic judge biases as a gallery of labeled cards, each pairing a one-glyph visual metaphor of the bias with its specific mitigation.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter itself calls bias 'the central challenge' and the mitigations 'the chapter's central skill', yet it is a long wall of prose subsections. A single at-a-glance card grid makes the bias -> mitigation mapping memorable and gives the reader a mental checklist. This is the highest-leverage intuition consolidation in the chapter.
new_figures.placement: Biases in LLM Judges: The Menagerie
new_figures.brief: A grid of five equal cards (e.g. a 3-over-2 or single-row-scrollable layout, whichever fits the viewBox without overlap), each with a bold title, a tiny schematic metaphor at top, a one-line 'symptom', and a one-line 'fix' in a distinct footer band. Card 1 POSITION BIAS: metaphor = two slots [A][B] with a thumb/weight tipping toward the first slot; symptom 'favors whichever response is shown first'; fix 'score both orderings, aggregate'. Card 2 VERBOSITY (LENGTH) BIAS: metaphor = a short bar vs a long bar with a crown auto-landing on the long one; symptom 'prefers longer answers regardless of quality'; fix 'anti-length instruction + conciseness criterion'. Card 3 SELF-PREFERENCE: metaphor = a judge glyph looking into a mirror that reflects a response of the same shape/family; symptom 'overrates its own model family'; fix 'judge with a different model family'. Card 4 SYCOPHANCY: metaphor = a response card with a confident/authoritative badge and the judge nodding along; symptom 'defers to confident tone or leaked labels'; fix 'neutral framing, never leak the human label'. Card 5 INSTRUCTION-FOLLOWING BIAS: metaphor = a checklist with all format boxes ticked but a small warning mark on a factual-error line; symptom 'rewards format compliance over accuracy'; fix 'separate, explicitly weighted rubric criteria'. Keep each card body in var(--v-fill) with var(--v-stroke) border; titles in var(--ink) / var(--accent); the 'fix' footer band tinted with a subtle var(--good) accent to read as the remedy; use var(--warn) sparingly on the symptom metaphors. No numbers needed beyond the small A/B slot labels. All five metaphors must be legible as simple line schematics, not detailed illustrations; ensure no text clipping and consistent card heights in both themes.

new_figures.name: judge-position-swap-debias
new_figures.concept: The swap-and-aggregate protocol that cancels position bias: run (A,B) and (B,A), flip the second result, and crown a winner only if both agree, else tie.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most-used, most-cited mitigation in the chapter (and the mechanism reused inside the automated-arena code). It is a small algorithm whose logic -- 'agreement across orderings survives, disagreement collapses to a tie' -- is far clearer as a two-lane diagram with a resolution table than as prose plus code. Foundational because position bias is the biggest pairwise pitfall.
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right flow. On the left, one input pair {question, Response A, Response B}. It fans into TWO horizontal lanes feeding the same 'Judge' box drawn twice (or one judge with two passes): TOP lane shows order (A then B) -> raw verdict e.g. 'says A'; BOTTOM lane shows the SWAPPED order (B then A) -> raw verdict e.g. 'says A' (i.e. the model again picked the first-shown slot). Add a small 'flip back to A/B space' relabeling node on the bottom lane so its verdict is expressed in true A/B terms (e.g. raw first-slot pick -> means 'B'). Both lanes converge into a RESOLVER box containing a compact 2x2-style rule: 'agree -> that winner' (good tint) and 'disagree -> tie' (warn/muted tint). Show the worked path: top says A, bottom (flipped) says B -> disagree -> output 'tie', with a caption 'the position-favored slot cancels'. Include a one-line footnote badge: 'cost x2, position bias ~eliminated'. Use var(--accent) for the Judge boxes, var(--good) for the agree branch, var(--muted)/var(--warn) for the tie branch, var(--v-fill)/var(--v-stroke) for input and resolver frames. Keep the flip/relabel step visually explicit -- that is the subtle part readers miss. Ensure arrows never cross-clutter; clear top-then-bottom reading order into the resolver.

new_figures.name: elo-battles-to-ranking
new_figures.concept: How a stream of noisy pairwise battles converges to a stable global Elo ranking: logistic expected-score from the rating gap, a surprise-scaled update per battle, and a leaderboard that reorders and stabilizes over many battles.
new_figures.rationale: The entire back half of the chapter (Chatbot Arena, automated arena, bootstrap CIs) rests on Elo, and the crucial intuition -- that many locally-noisy pairwise outcomes aggregate into a stable global skill estimate, with upsets moving ratings more than expected wins -- is inherently a process over time that a single static frame cannot fully convey. Motion shows the leaderboard settling and confidence tightening as battles accumulate, which is exactly the mental model needed. This is the one figure where animation is genuinely load-bearing.
new_figures.placement: Chatbot Arena and the Elo Rating System
new_figures.brief: Three coordinated regions; must be fully legible as a STATIC first frame, with animation layering the convergence story on top. REGION 1 (left) 'Expected score': the logistic curve P(A beats B) = 1 / (1 + 10^((theta_B - theta_A)/400)) drawn on axes x = rating gap (theta_A - theta_B) from about -400 to +400, y = win probability 0 to 1, with the midpoint marked at (0, 0.5) and a guide showing that a +400 gap ~ 0.9 (the '10:1' convention). Mark the current battle's gap on the curve with a dot reading off its expected probability. REGION 2 (center) 'One battle -> update': two model chips A (rating 1050) and B (rating 980); show expected P(A) ~ 0.6 pulled from the curve; show the actual outcome (A wins) and the update delta = K*(actual - expected) with K=32, so A: +12.8 -> 1062.8, B: -12.8 -> 967.2. Include a second, contrasting mini-callout: if the UNDERDOG B had won, the update is larger (~+19 for B) -- annotate 'upsets move ratings more'. REGION 3 (right) 'Leaderboard converges': a vertical bar/row ranking of ~4 illustrative models (label them Model 1..4, ratings near 1000 baseline, NO real model names, NO real leaderboard numbers) with small error whiskers. ANIMATION: battles stream in one at a time (a small pill '3 vs 1: 3 wins' flashing), each triggering (a) the dot sliding along the logistic curve to the new gap, (b) the two chips nudging by the surprise, (c) rows in the leaderboard swapping order early then settling, and (d) the error whiskers visibly SHRINKING as battle count climbs (show a 'battles: N' counter). End state: a stable order with tight whiskers; a final caption 'noisy pairwise battles -> stable global ranking'. Respect prefers-reduced-motion: the static frame must already show the logistic curve, the single worked update (1050/980, K=32, P~0.6, +/-12.8), and a settled leaderboard with whiskers. Use var(--accent) for the logistic curve and the active battle, var(--good)/var(--warn) or neutral fills for win/loss deltas, var(--muted) for whiskers/grid, var(--v-stroke)/var(--ink-soft) for axes and labels. Only the illustrative integers already in the chapter's worked example (1050, 980, 400, K=32, ~0.6, 12.8, 19); invent no benchmark ratings.

new_figures.name: judge-calibration-trust-loop
new_figures.concept: The audit cycle that earns trust in a judge: a gold human-labeled set gates the judge through agreement/Spearman thresholds before deployment, with a mandatory re-calibration loop whenever the judge model changes.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's thesis -- 'the judge is a tool with known failure modes, not a black box to trust blindly' -- lives in the calibration protocol, checklist, and interview answer, but is spread across three sections. A single loop diagram makes the cadence (build gold set -> measure -> gate -> deploy -> re-calibrate on model swap) intuitive and turns an abstract 'always validate' into a concrete cycle. Supports the key takeaway that no judge is trusted un-audited.
new_figures.brief: A clockwise cycle of four nodes with a gate. Node 1 'Gold set': ~300-500 (prompt, response) examples with majority-vote human labels, including a small 'adversarial' tag (long-but-wrong, short-but-right). Node 2 'Run judge': the judge scores the gold set. Node 3 'Measure': a compact metrics tile listing agreement rate, Spearman rho, MAE, and bias profile (per-ordering). Node 4 'GATE' drawn as a decision diamond: 'rho > 0.75 AND agreement > 70%?' -> PASS (good) arrow to a 'Deploy' node; FAIL (warn) arrow loops back to a 'Iterate judge prompt' node that returns to Run judge. From 'Deploy', a distinct dashed return arrow labeled 'judge model upgraded -> RE-CALIBRATE' loops all the way back to 'Run judge', emphasizing that a model swap re-opens the cycle. Use var(--good) for the PASS/deploy path, var(--warn) for the FAIL/iterate path, var(--accent) for the GATE diamond, var(--muted) dashed styling for the re-calibrate loop, var(--v-fill)/var(--v-stroke) for node frames. The thresholds 0.75 and 70% and the 300-500 count come straight from the chapter (illustrative, not invented benchmarks). Keep the cycle unambiguous -- clockwise arrows, the two branches off the gate clearly distinguished, no crossing clutter, legible in both themes.

summary: Concept-rich, figure-poor chapter (zero existing figures across ~800 lines). Proposed five foundational figures mapped to the core intuitions: (1) pointwise-vs-pairwise as the organizing dichotomy [P1, static]; (2) the five-bias menagerie with per-bias mitigations, the chapter's 'central skill' [P1, static]; (3) the swap-and-aggregate position-bias debiaser, the most-reused mitigation [P2, static]; (4) Elo battles-to-ranking, the one place where animation is load-bearing to show noisy pairwise outcomes converging to a stable global ranking [P1, long-animation]; and (5) the calibration/trust loop encoding the chapter's thesis that a judge must be audited against gold labels before and after any model swap [P3, static]. All briefs are theme-safe (semantic CSS vars, no hardcoded primaries), use only the chapter's own illustrative integers, and are fully legible as static frames.
